metadata.summary: Continuous-loop FinOps with AI agents: daily billing-snapshot agent, tag-auditor, idle-hunter, RI/SP-advisor, human-in-loop approval gates, and agent gotchas for safe cost automation.

content.title: FinOps with AI Agents: Continuous-Loop Cost Automation
content.summary: FinOps with agents is continuous-loop optimization: a daily billing-snapshot agent ingests cost data, a tag-auditor flags untagged resources, an idle-hunter files tickets for waste candidates, and a weekly RI/SP-advisor proposes commitment adjustments. All write-actions require human approval — agents never auto-delete or auto-terminate. Split detection (read-only) from remediation (write, gated) to prevent runaway savings loops.

why.p: Manual FinOps reviews happen monthly at best. Agentic workflows shift to continuous monitoring: anomalies are caught within hours, idle resources are surfaced weekly, and commitment coverage is reviewed against actual usage before the next billing cycle. The agent handles the data-intensive legwork; humans make the final call on consequential actions.

Agent-driven FinOps without guardrails produces the opposite of savings: an agent that both detects and remediates can terminate a production database because its CPU utilization looked "idle" (it was I/O-bound). Human-in-loop checkpoints on irreversible actions are the critical safety control.

list.item: Cloud bill above $10k/month and growing — manual weekly review misses too many anomalies.
Multiple teams deploying independently — no single human has visibility across all accounts.
Commitment (RI/SP) renewals require analysis of 30-90 day usage patterns — tedious for humans, trivial for agents.
Tagging compliance below 95% — tag-auditor agent closes the gap without manual resource-by-resource review.
Cloud bill below $5k/month — agent setup cost exceeds value; manual weekly review is sufficient.
Strictly regulated environments where all changes require formal change control — agent-generated tickets still need human workflow before approval.
No human owner available to review agent recommendations — agents without a human approver accumulate unreviewed backlogs that become stale and misleading.

rule.statement: Split FinOps agent responsibility into two distinct agents per domain: (1) a detection agent (read-only, runs on schedule, produces structured findings) and (2) a remediation agent (write-access, runs only on human-approved tickets, applies a single specific action). Never combine detection and remediation in one agent — prevents runaway savings loops where the agent applies its own findings without human review.
rule.rationale: Detection-remediation conflation is the most dangerous FinOps agent failure mode. A detection agent that also remediates can terminate resources in a feedback loop before any human sees the findings. Read-only detection agents are safe to run continuously; remediation agents should be triggered only by human-approved tickets.

rule.statement: Required agentic FinOps loop: (1) daily billing-snapshot agent ingests CUR/billing export → writes to queryable store (Athena/BigQuery/Snowflake); (2) daily tag-auditor agent flags untagged resources by spend with owner-best-guess from CloudTrail; (3) weekly idle-hunter agent finds candidates and files tickets/PRs — never auto-deletes; (4) weekly RI/SP-advisor runs against last 30/90-day usage and proposes commitments. Always require human approval for any action with spend impact above configured threshold.
rule.rationale: The four-agent loop covers the complete FinOps lifecycle: visibility (snapshot), tagging (auditor), waste (idle-hunter), commitments (advisor). Each agent is single-purpose and read-only except when executing a human-approved action.

list.item: finops-tag-auditor (Sonnet, read-only) — given billing export and tag policy, lists untagged resources by spend with owner-best-guess from CloudTrail.
finops-rightsizing-advisor (Opus) — given utilization metrics, proposes instance-class changes with risk/saving table.

finops-idle-hunter (Sonnet, read-only) — finds unattached EBS volumes, unused Elastic IPs, idle NAT gateways, stopped instances older than 7 days, orphaned snapshots; outputs structured ticket list with rollback commands.
finops-ri-sp-advisor (Opus) — analyzes last 30/90-day usage patterns, current commitments, growth forecast; proposes RI/SP purchases with expected savings and flexibility tradeoffs.

section.name: idle-hunter-script
section.p: Reference implementation — AWS unattached EBS volume finder:

example.title: AWS idle EBS volume finder (read-only, under 40 lines)
example.note: Read-only; produces JSON ticket list. Human reviews the list and approves actions individually. Never wire this directly to a delete command.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.prompt: Generate weekly FinOps report for <account/org>. Inputs: 7d + 28d billing exports, tag policy, prior recommendations, current commitments. Output: (1) top 10 cost movers (week-over-week, with attribution), (2) untagged resource spend, (3) idle-resource candidates with reclaim impact ($, risk), (4) RI/SP coverage + recommendation for next purchase, (5) AI-workload cost per 1k requests, (6) anomaly list with confidence score. Forbid: any auto-delete; produce tickets/PRs only; mark every recommendation with a reversibility flag (1-way / 2-way door).

Idle-resource analysis. For each resource: (id, type, account, owner_tag, monthly_cost, last_activity, evidence_metric, recommended_action, blast_radius, rollback_command). Flag any without owner_tag for tagging triage.

Generate an executive summary of cloud costs for [month/quarter]:
Data: [Paste cost data by service, team, environment]
Include: 1. One-paragraph executive summary 2. Key metrics table (total spend, vs budget, vs last period) 3. Top 3 highlights (positive or concerning) 4. Top 3 action items 5. Forecast for next period
Keep language business-focused, avoid technical jargon. Maximum length: 1 page.

Analyze Reserved Instance / Savings Plan underutilization:
Commitment details: - Type: [RI/Savings Plan] - Term: [1/3 years] - Purchase date: [date] - Monthly commitment: $[amount] - Current utilization: [X]%
Usage data: [Paste utilization metrics]
Provide: 1. Root cause of underutilization 2. Options to improve utilization 3. Cost of continuing current state 4. Recommendations (modify, exchange, sell, accept) 5. Prevention strategies for future purchases

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.warning: Human-in-loop checkpoint is mandatory for any action that terminates resources, modifies Reserved Instances or Savings Plans, or changes instance families. Agent emits the ticket; human signs off; agent applies after explicit approval. This is a non-negotiable safety control, not a convenience feature.

antipattern.description: Agent identifies "low CPU" resources as idle and proposes rightsizing or termination.
antipattern.reason: A service may be I/O-bound, GPU-bound, or network-bound; CPU utilization alone is a dangerously incomplete signal. Rightsizing on CPU alone breaks workloads. Always include memory, network, GPU, and disk IOPS before classifying a resource as idle.

antipattern.description: LLM proposes spot instances for stateful workloads ("save 70%!") without confirming checkpointing is implemented.
antipattern.reason: Spot interruption on a stateful workload without checkpointing produces a data-loss incident. Force the agent to check for checkpoint configuration before recommending spot, and mark the recommendation blocked until checkpointing is confirmed.

antipattern.description: RI/SP recommendations from LLMs that assume usage continues linearly.
antipattern.reason: LLMs extrapolate from recent history without accounting for planned workload changes, seasonal patterns, or architecture migrations. Force the agent to state the assumption explicitly: "this assumes above-70% steady-state usage continues for 12 months." Human signs off on the assumption, not just the recommendation.

antipattern.description: Tag-fix automations rewriting owner tags based on CloudTrail data.
antipattern.reason: The original creator may have left the company; stale CloudTrail data produces wrong ownership attribution. Cross-reference HRIS or Backstage service catalog before assigning owner tags retroactively. Manual confirmation for any unverifiable ownership.

antipattern.description: Auto-shutdown of "idle" non-prod environments at a fixed UTC time.
antipattern.reason: A 18:00 UTC shutdown hits an APAC team's morning standup. Add timezone awareness to shutdown schedules — either per-region cron offsets or a timezone metadata tag on the resource. Query team timezone from a service catalog, not hardcode UTC.

antipattern.description: Anomaly detection agents claiming spikes on less than 60 days of history.
antipattern.reason: Monthly batch jobs, billing cycle artifacts, and seasonal patterns all look like anomalies to a detector trained on short history. Train anomaly detectors on at least 60 days (two full billing cycles) before using them to page on-call.

rule.statement: Tag policy first, optimization second. Without owner, cost-center, env, and data-class on more than 95% of resources, every report is fiction. Run the tag-auditor agent before any other FinOps agent.
rule.rationale: All downstream FinOps automation — showback, chargeback, idle detection, rightsizing — depends on reliable tag data. Optimize tagging before optimizing cost.

rule.statement: Showback (visibility) before Chargeback (accountability) before Optimization (action). Skipping showback breaks engineering trust in the numbers before they have a chance to validate them.
rule.rationale: Teams resist costs they cannot verify. Showback builds the shared understanding of cost attribution; chargeback converts that understanding into accountability. Optimization actions taken before trust is established produce organizational resistance that costs more than the savings.

rule.statement: Commit to Reserved Instances and Savings Plans at 60-70% of baseline usage coverage — never 100%. The remaining 30-40% headroom accommodates autoscaling, burst traffic, and new workloads without paying on-demand for coverage gaps. Reassess commitment coverage quarterly.
rule.rationale: Over-commitment locks in spend for workloads that may be retired or rightsized. Under-commitment leaves money on the table. The 60-70% floor is the empirically validated FinOps industry target for balancing savings against flexibility.

rule.statement: For AI costs: always track $/request, $/1k tokens (input and output separately), $/training-run, and cache-hit-rate. Without per-request metrics, GPU spend is opaque. Instance-hour reports mask token-level cost drivers.
rule.rationale: A model upgrade that doubles output token count per request doubles inference cost even if GPU utilization stays flat. Only per-token tracking catches this before it compounds over weeks.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Agent-friendly CLI tools for FinOps automation:

list.item: aws ce — Cost Explorer: cost queries and forecasts. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/ce/
aws cur + S3 + Athena — Cost and Usage Report: single source of truth for detailed billing. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cur/
aws compute-optimizer — rightsizing recommendations. https://aws.amazon.com/compute-optimizer/
gcloud billing / BigQuery billing export — GCP detailed billing. https://cloud.google.com/billing/docs/how-to/export-data-bigquery
gcloud recommender — idle and rightsizing recommendations. https://cloud.google.com/recommender/docs
az consumption / Cost Management exports — Azure billing. https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/
kubectl-cost (Kubecost CLI) — Kubernetes cost allocation. https://www.kubecost.com/
infracost — IaC cost diff in PRs. https://www.infracost.io/
FOCUS spec exporters — vendor-neutral billing format. https://focus.finops.org/
